itineraries.title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
itineraries.subtitle: Two weeks swapping colonial backstreets for secret beaches — from the Las Terrazas eco-village to the dunes of Playa Pilar, with a stop at the Bay of Pigs cenotes along the way.
specialties: Ropa vieja and congrí (slow-cooked creole cuisine)
Papier-mâché and wood-carved Che figurines (Trinidad craft markets)
Guarapo (fresh sugar cane juice, sold at roadside stands)

map_keywords: Plaza de la Catedral La Habana

visits.text: Old Havana (UNESCO 1982), founded in 1519, packs nearly a thousand historically significant buildings into just 5 km². Wander the Plaza de la Catedral — a baroque cathedral dating to 1777 with mismatched asymmetrical towers — then stroll the 460-metre pedestrian artery of Calle Obispo before reaching Plaza de Armas, where the Palacio de los Capitanes Generales (1776) still has its original wooden cobblestones, laid to muffle the sound of carriages and spare the governor his sleep.

El Capitolio (1926–1929), 207 metres long, shelters a 25-carat diamond beneath its 92-metre dome — the official kilometre zero of every road in Cuba. Its bronze Statue of the Republic (14.6 m tall, 30 tonnes) was the third-largest indoor statue in the world when it was unveiled.

activities.text: Arrival and transfer from the airport area (about 30 minutes). As the day winds down, head straight to the Malecón for your first taste of Havana: a 8-km sea-front promenade built between 1901 and 1958, nicknamed 'the world's longest sofa.' At 9 pm, don't miss the cañonazo — a cannon fired nightly from the La Cabaña fortress across the bay.

map_keywords: Baños del Río San Juan Las Terrazas

visits.text: Las Terrazas is a 1,000-person eco-village founded in 1968 out of an extraordinary act of ecological repair: after centuries of deforestation caused by coffee plantations and charcoal production, seven million trees — mahogany, cedar, teak — were replanted across 5,000 hectares of hillside terraces carved by hand (hence the name). In 1985, the Sierra del Rosario was designated Cuba's first UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. Today the community, built around an artificial lake, earns 60% of its income from eco-tourism.

Cafetal Buenavista is the ruin of a coffee plantation founded in the early 19th century by French colonists who fled the Haitian Revolution. The stone drying terraces, slave quarters and manor house still stand. At the region's peak, there were 54 plantations like this one — Buenavista is among the oldest survivors.

The Orchid Garden at Soroa, 20 km from Las Terrazas, was created in the 1940s by a Canarian lawyer in memory of his wife and daughter. Spread across 35,000 m² of mountain terraces, it brings together over 700 orchid species — including 250 endemic to Cuba.

activities.text: Swim in the Baños del Río San Juan — a series of natural pools and small waterfalls deep in the tropical forest, 3 km from Las Terrazas. Cool, clear freshwater flows between moss-covered rocks.

Guided hike through the Sierra del Rosario: over 120 bird species live here, including the tocororo — Cuba's national bird, wearing the red, white and blue of the Cuban flag — and the tiny, jewel-bright Cuban tody, a red-and-green endemic found nowhere else on earth.

practical_info.booking: Guide required — book at Hotel Moka

visits.text: The Viñales Valley (UNESCO 1999) covers 132 km² in the Sierra de los Órganos, a landscape dotted with mogotes — sheer-sided Jurassic limestone formations up to 300 metres tall, some 150 million years old. Spanish sailors thought they looked like organ pipes, giving the sierra its name. The province of Pinar del Río grows around 80% of Cuba's tobacco using entirely traditional methods: ox-drawn ploughs, leaf-by-leaf harvesting, and 45–60 days of curing in wooden tobacco barns.

The Mural of Prehistory (120 m high, 180 m wide), painted on the face of the Pita mogote between 1961 and 1965 by Leovigildo González Morillo, traces the evolution of life in the sierra. Continue to the Mirador de Los Jazmines for a sweeping panoramic view across the entire valley.

activities.text: Horseback ride through the Valle del Silencio, passing between mogotes, tobacco fields and cassava plantations. In season (January–March), pickers work the rows by hand. A local farmer will brew you fresh coffee and roll a cigar in front of you — the real thing.

visits.text: Cayo Levisa, a small island in the Colorados archipelago, can only be reached by ferry from Palma Rubia — 45 minutes by road from Viñales. Ferries depart at 10am and 6pm, with return crossings at 9am and 5pm (30-minute trip). The island's 3-km white-sand beach is almost completely deserted: there is just one hotel here, with 40 bungalows. The reef, 2 km offshore, shelters 47 of the 59 coral species recorded in Cuba and more than 120 fish species.

activities.text: Full-day trip to Cayo Levisa: take the 10am ferry, spend the day swimming and snorkelling, and return on the 5pm boat. The island's dive centre offers excursions to 23 different dive sites. Kayaks and snorkelling gear are available to rent on the beach.

practical_info.booking: Book through Cubanacan agency in Viñales, or arrange independently

visits.text: A long driving day: leave Viñales early, cross the plains of Pinar del Río and skirt Havana on the national motorway (about 5h30 on the road). You'll arrive at Playa Girón in the early afternoon. This coastal village takes its name from Gilberto Girón, a French pirate who was beheaded here in the 17th century — though it is better known for the failed Bay of Pigs landing on 17 April 1961, when 1,400 CIA-trained Cuban exiles tried to topple the Castro regime and were routed in 72 hours. The museum displays photos, weapons and documents from this pivotal Cold War episode.

La Cueva de los Peces, halfway between Playa Larga and Playa Girón, is Cuba's deepest cenote: 70 metres of crystal-clear freshwater filling a tectonic fissure connected to the sea by underwater tunnels. Tropical fish drift through the clear water. The adjacent beach is ideal for snorkelling directly from the shore.

activities.text: Late-afternoon swim in the turquoise waters of the Bay of Pigs. Underwater visibility here ranges from 20 to 40 metres, with water temperatures between 22°C and 29°C depending on the season.

visits.text: Morning snorkelling at Punta Perdiz, 10 km north of Playa Girón and considered the Bay of Pigs' finest spot: a coral reef just 100 metres from shore, alive with damselfish, wrasse and Bermuda chubs.

Drive to Cienfuegos (about 1h30). Cienfuegos (UNESCO 2005), known as the 'Pearl of the South', is the only Cuban city founded by the French: on 22 April 1819, Louis de Clouet and 36 Bordeaux colonists laid out its first streets around Parque José Martí in a perfect grid. The neoclassical facades and the extraordinary Palacio de Valle (1913–1917) — a Moorish-Gothic-baroque hybrid built by an Asturian sugar baron — set this city apart from anywhere else in Cuba.

activities.text: Evening stroll along the Cienfuegos Malecón overlooking one of the world's largest natural bays, then watch the sunset from the rooftop terrace of the Palacio de Valle.

visits.text: Trinidad (UNESCO 1988), founded in 1514, is the best-preserved colonial town in Cuba. The Plaza Mayor is ringed by the mansions of 18th- and 19th-century sugar barons: the Museo Romántico (Palacio Brunet, 1812) holds mahogany furniture, Bohemian crystal chandeliers and Sèvres porcelain. Climb the bell tower of the San Francisco convent (1813) for a 360° view over the red-tiled rooftops, the Escambray mountains and the Caribbean Sea.

activities.text: Wander the cobblestone streets lined with pastel-coloured houses. Trinidad looks much as it did in the 19th century: no traffic lights, horse-drawn carts, and interior courtyards glimpsed through open doorways. At night, the bars around Plaza Mayor spill salsa out onto the street — the Casa de la Música, perched at the top of a monumental staircase, is the place to be.

visits.text: The Valley of the Sugar Mills (UNESCO), 12 km from Trinidad, once held more than 50 sugar mills and 11,000 enslaved people in the 19th century. The Torre Manaca Iznaga (1815–1830, 45 metres, seven storeys) was used to watch over enslaved workers and ring out the hours of labour. From the top, views stretch across the ruined plantation estates to the Escambray massif. The Iznaga family — Creoles of Basque descent — were among the most powerful in the region.

activities.text: Excursion through the valley by tourist train or car, with stops at the Torre Manaca Iznaga and the Casa Guachinango, an 18th-century cattle hacienda now serving as a riverside restaurant on the Ay river — swimming possible.

visits.text: Topes de Collantes is a natural park sitting at 800 metres in the Sierra del Escambray. A trail winds down to the Salto del Caburní, a 62-metre waterfall dropping into a natural pool. The surrounding humid tropical forest is a tangle of wild coffee trees, giant bamboo and three-metre ferns.
Playa Ancón (12 km south of Trinidad), widely considered the finest beach on Cuba's south coast: 4 km of white sand, calm deep-blue water, and a coral reef within easy snorkelling distance.

activities.text: Morning hike down to the Salto del Caburní, followed by a swim in the natural rock pools at its base. The return is by the same trail — the climb back up is steep.
Afternoon on the beach at Playa Ancón. Boat trips out to the reef are available on site.

visits.text: A long morning drive from Trinidad to the cayos via Santa Clara (about 4h30). The northern coast islands (Coco, Guillermo) are reached via a 17-km causeway built over the open sea. Cayo Guillermo is wilder than its neighbour Cayo Coco, and was the favourite hideout of a famous American writer who came here to fish for marlin in the 1940s and 1950s.

activities.text: Arrive in the early afternoon, settle in, then head straight to Playa Pilar — the beach many Cubans call the most beautiful in the country: blinding white sand, dunes up to 15 metres high (the tallest in the Caribbean), and shallow turquoise water. The beach stays quiet because it sits at the far western tip of the island.

map_keywords: Parque Natural El Bagá Cayo Coco

visits.text: A full day on the cayos. Beyond Playa Pilar, neighbouring Cayo Coco — linked by road — has its own fine-sand beaches fringed with palm trees and flamingo colonies in the interior lagoons. The El Bagá natural park showcases Cuba's endemic wildlife in a mangrove setting.

activities.text: Snorkelling on the coral reef off Cayo Guillermo, reached by organised boat excursion (reefs lie 200–500 m offshore). There is also the option to swim with dolphins at a natural dolphinarium.

visits.text: Remedios, founded in 1514, is one of Cuba's oldest towns — and one of its least known. Its Plaza Martí is the only square in Cuba with two churches facing each other. The Iglesia San Juan Bautista (16th century, rebuilt in the 18th) hides a remarkable secret: an altar covered entirely in gold leaf, forgotten under layers of paint for centuries and only rediscovered during a restoration in the 1940s.

The Museo de las Parrandas documents the 'parrandas' — raucous street festivals held every 24 December since 1820, in which two rival neighbourhoods (San Salvador and El Carmen) compete by building enormous illuminated floats and staging fireworks battles. A tradition unique to Cuba, now inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list.

activities.text: Wander through Remedios, a town that mass tourism has barely touched. Low pastel-coloured houses, flower-filled courtyards, rocking chairs on front porches. The atmosphere of Trinidad, without the crowds.

map_keywords: Fortaleza San Carlos de la Cabaña La Habana
Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes La Habana
Plaza de la Revolución La Habana

visits.text: Back to Havana (about 4 hours by road). In the afternoon, visit the Fortaleza de San Carlos de la Cabaña (1763–1774), the largest fortress in Latin America (700 metres long), built after the British captured Havana in 1762. Every evening at 9pm, soldiers in 18th-century uniform fire the cañonazo — a tradition Havana has kept alive for over 250 years.

Plaza de la Revolución: 72,000 m² of open space, anchored by the José Martí Memorial (109 m, built in the shape of a five-pointed star) and the giant steel outline of Che Guevara on the Interior Ministry facade. Take the lift to the top of the memorial — the highest viewpoint in Havana.

activities.text: Last evening in Havana: walk through the Vedado neighbourhood, a showcase of 1930s–1950s Art Deco and modernist architecture, and catch one final sunset over the Malecón.
Transfer to the airport area and departure formalities. Allow 30 minutes from central Havana.

seo.h1_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
seo.intro_paragraph: Two weeks moving between colonial backstreets and off-the-map beaches: the Las Terrazas eco-village deep in the forest, the deserted island of Cayo Levisa, the cenotes of the Bay of Pigs, the sand dunes of Playa Pilar and the forgotten town of Remedios.
seo.seo_hook: Forget Varadero and its concrete hotels. This route alternates colonial towns with secret beaches: an island only reachable by ferry, a 70-metre cenote connected to the sea by underwater tunnels, 15-metre sand dunes on a nearly deserted cayo, and an eco-village born from seven million replanted trees.
featured_list: Las Terrazas: UNESCO eco-village planted from seven million trees
Cayo Levisa: a deserted island reachable only by ferry
Bay of Pigs: cenotes, snorkelling and Cold War history
Playa Pilar: 15-metre dunes on a near-virgin cayo
Remedios: a 1514 town famous for its unique Christmas festivals

places_taglines.CU::la-havane-vieja: Where a thousand baroque and Art Deco buildings rise from streets unchanged since 1519
places_taglines.CU::soroa: Seven hundred orchid species tucked into a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in the mountains
places_taglines.CU::las-terrazas: A thousand-person community born from the planting of seven million trees
places_taglines.CU::vinales: Jurassic limestone towers and hand-rolled cigars in a UNESCO valley
places_taglines.CU::cayo-levisa: Three kilometres of white sand, 47 coral species, and a ferry as the only way in
places_taglines.CU::playa-giron: Where Cold War history and Caribbean waters meet on the Bay of Pigs
places_taglines.CU::cienfuegos: Cuba's only French-founded city — neoclassical boulevards on one of the world's great natural bays
places_taglines.CU::trinidad: A 1514 colonial town frozen in time, where salsa pours out of doorways every night
places_taglines.CU::cayo-guillermo: The Caribbean's tallest sand dunes and Hemingway's favourite fishing waters
places_taglines.CU::remedios: A gold-altared church, Christmas fireworks wars and zero tourist crowds
places_taglines.CU::la-havane-vedado: Art Deco Havana facing the sea — the city's modernist soul, lit up at sunset
places_taglines.CU::jose-marti: Gateway to the world from José Martí International Airport

highlights: Culture and beach alternate every 1–2 days throughout the route
Las Terrazas: Cuba's first eco-village, UNESCO Biosphere Reserve
Cayo Levisa: 47 coral species, accessible only by ferry
Bay of Pigs: a 70-metre cenote, exceptional snorkelling and a Cold War museum
Playa Pilar: 15-metre sand dunes, the beach Cubans themselves call the finest in the country
Remedios: an authentic colonial town almost untouched by tourism

seo.h1_title: Classic Cuba Tour: Havana, Viñales Valley & Colonial Trinidad Itinerary
seo.intro_paragraph: Havana, Viñales and its tobacco fields, Cienfuegos, Trinidad, Camagüey and Varadero. Three weeks to discover Cuba, its vintage cars, its music and its soul.
seo.seo_hook: Vintage cars rumble through the streets, salsa pulses every night, tobacco smoke drifts through the valleys. Cuba is a living, magnificent time capsule.

seo.h1_title: The Classic Cuba Circuit: A Timeless Road Trip from Havana to Trinidad
seo.intro_paragraph: Havana and its vintage cars, Viñales mogotes, Cienfuegos, colonial Trinidad, Santa Clara and its revolutionary history, Varadero beaches. In Cuba, time has stopped and salsa sets the rhythm.
seo.seo_hook: Vintage cars rumble through the streets, salsa pulses every night, tobacco smoke drifts through the valleys. Cuba is a living, magnificent time capsule.
